metadata.summary: Entry-level accessibility layer: WCAG POUR principles, conformance levels, evaluation types, quick-check protocol, and pre-commit gate criteria.

content.title: Accessibility Basics
content.summary: The entry-level accessibility layer for web products: WCAG POUR principles, A/AA/AAA conformance levels, four evaluation types, five-minute quick-check protocol, and pre-commit gate criteria. Automated scanners catch only ~30% of issues; manual and assistive-tech testing is required.

why.p: Products without accessibility baseline exclude users with disabilities, create legal compliance risk (ADA, Section 508, AODA), and accumulate expensive late-stage fixes. Wiring axe-core into CI catches regressions at PR time, where fixing a failing input label costs minutes rather than hours in a QA cycle. The POUR framework gives teams a shared vocabulary for categorizing and prioritizing issues.

list.item: Onboarding a team or codebase to accessibility for the first time
Pre-launch sanity sweep on a small site or feature where a full WCAG 2.2 conformance audit is overkill
Wiring CI to catch the obvious 30% automated tools find: missing alt, unlabelled inputs, contrast failures, heading skips
Educating a new agent or developer before they edit UI code
Final compliance sign-off — use wcag-22-compliance and regulatory-compliance-2026
Real assistive-technology testing flows (NVDA/VoiceOver/TalkBack) — use testing-with-assistive-technology
Procurement / VPAT-ACR generation — use regulatory-compliance-2026
XR/spatial experiences — use vr-design-patterns, ar-design-patterns, spatial-accessibility

section.name: WCAG POUR Principles
section.p: POUR is a teaching frame for categorizing accessibility issues, not a checklist. Treating it as a checklist produces shallow audits.

list.item: Perceivable: can users perceive all content? Examples: alt text for images, captions for video, colour contrast of 4.5:1 for text and 3:1 for UI components.
Operable: can users operate all controls? Examples: full keyboard access, no keyboard traps, sufficient time limits, no seizure-inducing flashes.
Understandable: can users understand content and UI? Examples: clear language, predictable navigation, error identification and suggestion.
Robust: does it work with current and future assistive technology? Examples: valid HTML, correct ARIA usage, name-role-value on all custom components.
Level A: minimum accessibility — must have. Blocking issues for all users with disabilities.
Level AA: standard for most products — legal requirement in most jurisdictions (ADA, Section 508, AODA, EN 301 549). Target for all public-facing products.
Level AAA: enhanced accessibility — ideal but not always achievable for all content types; target where possible.

rule.statement: Pin "WCAG 2.2 AA" in every prompt and audit specification. Agents conflate WCAG levels and produce mixed-level audits that obscure compliance status.
rule.rationale: WCAG 2.2 adds four new success criteria not in 2.1; specifying the version prevents auditing against an outdated standard.

rule.statement: Automated scanning catches approximately 30% of accessibility issues. Manual testing catches approximately 50%. Assistive-technology testing and user testing with disabled users are required for the remaining issues.
rule.rationale: Agents overweight scanner output; the 30% ceiling is explicit and must be stated whenever scanner results are reported.

list.item: Automated testing (axe, pa11y, Lighthouse): continuous, catches structural issues (missing alt, label, contrast, heading order, duplicate IDs).
Manual testing with keyboard-only navigation: catches focus traps, invisible focus indicators, interaction-order issues.
Assistive technology testing (NVDA, VoiceOver, TalkBack): catches ARIA misuse, dynamic content not announced, custom widget behavior.
User testing with disabled participants: catches task-completion barriers that no tool or heuristic predicts.

section.name: Five-Minute Quick-Check Protocol
section.p: Run before any PR that touches UI:

list.item: Tab through the page: can you reach every interactive element? Is the focus indicator always visible?
Zoom to 200%: is everything still usable? Does content reflow without horizontal scroll?
Check one image: does the alt text convey the same information as the image?
Check one form: are all labels visible, connected to inputs, and descriptive?
Run axe DevTools: are there any critical or serious violations?

rule.statement: Every form input must have a visible, programmatically associated label. Placeholder text is not a label substitute: it disappears when typing and has insufficient contrast.
rule.rationale: Screen readers announce the associated label on focus; without it, blind users cannot determine the field's purpose. Low-contrast placeholders fail SC 1.4.3.

example.note: No label; placeholder disappears on input; screen reader announces only type.

example.code: We'll never share your email.
example.note: Visible label, programmatic association, hint text linked via aria-describedby.

rule.statement: Decorative images: empty alt="" plus role="presentation". Informative images: concise description of the information conveyed. Complex images: short alt plus a long description linked via aria-describedby.
rule.rationale: Alt text must convey the same information as the image to a user who cannot see it; the correct pattern depends on the image's role in the page.

antipattern.description: Adding aria-label to a native element that already has an accessible name from its label element.
antipattern.reason: Duplicate accessible names confuse screen readers; the aria-label overrides the visible label, causing inconsistency for sighted screen-reader users.

antipattern.description: Using role="button" on a div element.
antipattern.reason: Native <button> provides keyboard activation (Enter and Space), implicit role, and focus management for free; div + role requires manual keyboard handler and is frequently incomplete.

antipattern.description: Adding ARIA attributes to fix scanner failures without understanding the semantic model.
antipattern.reason: Bad ARIA is worse than no ARIA; it creates a false accessible name or role that misleads assistive technology into announcing incorrect information.

section.name: CI Gate

rule.statement: Wire axe-core into PR CI. A failing PR for a missing label is cheaper than a post-launch fix. Lint with eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y to catch issues at type time.
rule.rationale: Accessibility debt compounds; catching issues at PR time costs minutes, at QA costs hours, and post-launch can cost litigation.

section.name: Agent Integration
section.p: Use as the on-ramp / triage layer: a fast agent runs axe-core + pa11y over routes, plus heuristic checks against the quick checklist (Tab path, 200% zoom, alt text sanity, label binding, heading order). It produces a categorized issue list (perceivable / operable / understandable / robust), routes critical issues to fix-PR agents, and escalates anything ambiguous (ARIA, complex components, dynamic regions) to human accessibility review or to testing-with-assistive-technology.

list.item: faion-accessibility-specialist-agent — POUR-categorized triage and remediation suggestions.
faion-frontend-developer-agent — apply fixes (alt, label, contrast, heading hierarchy, focus styles).
faion-testing-developer-agent — wire axe-core / pa11y-ci / lighthouse-ci into CI; gate PRs on regressions.
faion-ux-ui-designer-agent — design-token review for contrast, focus indicator, target size before code lands.

section.name: Prompt Patterns
section.p: Input: URL list or HTML snapshot.

Run conceptual checks against POUR: missing alt, unlabelled inputs, contrast <4.5:1 (text) / 3:1 (UI), heading skips, keyboard-only path broken, focus invisible, duplicate IDs, language not set. Return JSON {issue_id, principle, severity: critical/major/minor, selector, fix, wcag_ref}.

list.item: 1) Tab from top → list focus path + first unreachable element.
2) Zoom 200% → list reflow breakages.
3) Pick one img → critique alt text.
4) Pick one form → list label/aria-describedby gaps.
5) axe scan → report critical violations only.
axe-core — Programmatic a11y checks (in-browser, Node, Playwright, Puppeteer). npm i -D axe-core @axe-core/playwright
pa11y / pa11y-ci — CLI runner over URLs, JSON output, CI integration. npm i -D pa11y-ci
lighthouse-ci — Aggregate perf + a11y + SEO with budgets in CI. npm i -D @lhci/cli
axe-cli — CLI-only axe runner. npm i -g @axe-core/cli
wcag-zoo — Heading-order + skip-link checks, simple Python. pip install wcag-zoo
eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y — Lint React/JSX for known a11y mistakes. npm i -D eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y
eslint-plugin-vuejs-accessibility — Same for Vue. npm i -D eslint-plugin-vuejs-accessibility
headingsmap (browser ext) — Manual heading-tree review. Chrome/Firefox stores
Deque axe DevTools / Auditor — SaaS + browser ext. Yes — REST + CLI. Most accurate scanner; paid tiers expand coverage
WAVE (WebAIM) — SaaS + browser ext. Limited — UI-centric. Strong educational visualization
SiteImprove — SaaS. Yes — REST. Enterprise crawl + remediation tracking
Tenon — SaaS. Yes — REST. API-first scanner
Accessibility Insights for Web (Microsoft) — OSS. Partial — assessment-driven. Guided manual + automated
Pa11y Dashboard — OSS, self-host. Yes. Tracks scans over time
Tota11y — OSS bookmarklet. Manual. Visual overlay for first-pass review
Wire axe-core into PR CI early; failing PR is cheaper than a lawsuit.
Lint with eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y (or framework equivalent) in editor — catch issues at type time.
Build the team habit: every PR that touches UI runs the 5-minute manual test before review.
Use semantic HTML first; reach for ARIA only when no semantic equivalent exists.
Keep an a11y backlog separate from feature backlog — visible debt, owned, not buried.
Train designers on contrast (4.5:1 text, 3:1 UI) and focus visibility — most bugs originate in design tokens.
Agents add aria-label to native elements that already have accessible names — duplicates and confuses screen readers.
Agents recommend role="button" on <div> instead of using <button> — keyboard handlers go missing.
Agents conflate WCAG levels (A vs. AA vs. AAA); pin "WCAG 2.2 AA" in every prompt.
Color-contrast checks fail on text over images / gradients; agents miss this — escalate to manual review.
Generated alt text from filenames is bad; require model to read surrounding context before authoring alt.
Agents skip cognitive accessibility; explicitly include "plain language, predictable, error-prevention" in audit scope.
Human-in-loop checkpoints: ARIA additions, custom-component patterns (combobox, tabs, modal), focus-management refactors, anything dynamic with live regions.

template.purpose: Playwright + axe-core CI gate for WCAG 2.2 AA across defined routes
